input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **system_under_study** in the provided documents primarily focuses on **cyber-biophysical systems** (Document [7]) and **automotive manufacturing systems** (Documents [8], [9], [10], [12], [14], [17]). Key characteristics include:

1. **Cyber-Biophysical Systems (Document [7])**:
   - **Type**: Hybrid systems integrating **physical, biological, and cyber components** (e.g., IoT-enabled sensors, actuators, and biological processes).
   - **Main Components**:
     - **Physical Layer**: Sensors (e.g., temperature, pressure, humidity), actuators, and embedded systems.
     - **Biological Layer**: Living organisms or bio-processes (e.g., microbial cultures, plant growth systems).
     - **Cyber Layer**: Edge/Cloud computing platforms, AI/ML models, and real-time data processing pipelines.
   - **Operational Domain**: **Industrial IoT (IIoT), smart manufacturing, and environmental monitoring** with bidirectional data flow between physical and digital domains.
   - **Key Characteristics**:
     - **Dynamic heterogeneity**: Real-time adaptation to biological/physical variability (e.g., microbial growth rates, environmental changes).
     - **Multi-scale modeling**: Integration of **microscopic (e.g., cellular processes) and macroscopic (e.g., system-wide performance)** data.
     - **Feedback loops**: Closed-loop control via digital twin simulations to optimize biological/physical outcomes (e.g., yield prediction in bioreactors).
     - **Regulatory compliance**: Alignment with **ISO/IEC 30173** (Digital Twin terminology) and **ISO 23247-1** (manufacturing frameworks) for standardization.

2. **Automotive Manufacturing Systems (Documents [8], [9], [10], [12], [14], [17])**:
   - **Type**: **Industrial production lines** (e.g., vehicle assembly, battery systems) with **physical assets** (machines, robots, conveyors) and **digital counterparts**.
   - **Main Components**:
     - **Physical Layer**: CNC machines, robotic arms, assembly lines, and battery cells/modules.
     - **Cyber Layer**: PLCs (Programmable Logic Controllers), SCADA systems, and **edge/Cloud platforms** for real-time monitoring.
     - **Digital Layer**: **Digital twins of individual components** (e.g., battery cells) and **system-level twins** (e.g., entire production line).
   - **Operational Domain**: **Smart factories** with **predictive maintenance, quality control, and energy optimization**.
   - **Key Characteristics**:
     - **Modularity**: Support for **plug-and-play digital twins** of components (e.g., ISO 23247-1 compliant modular architectures).
     - **Real-time synchronization**: **Sub-second latency** for bidirectional data exchange (e.g., via OPC UA or MQTT protocols).
     - **AI-driven simulation**: Use of **physics-based and data-driven models** (e.g., finite element analysis for battery degradation) integrated with **machine learning** for predictive analytics.
     - **Energy efficiency**: Optimization of **power consumption** in manufacturing processes via digital twin simulations (e.g., Document [14]).
     - **Battery-specific twins** (Document [17]): Focus on **state-of-health (SoH) and state-of-charge (SoC) modeling** for lithium-ion batteries, including **thermal and electrochemical simulations**.

**Shared Cross-Domain Characteristics**:
   - **Standardization**: Adherence to **ISO/IEC 30173** (concepts and terminology) and **ISO 23247-1** (manufacturing framework) for interoperability.
   - **Data Fusion**: Combination of **sensor data, simulation outputs, and historical logs** via **ETL pipelines** (e.g., Apache NiFi, Kafka).
   - **Scalability**: Support for **hierarchical digital twins** (e.g., component → subsystem → system) as described in **Systems of Twinned Systems** (Document [13]).
   - **Adaptability**: Dynamic reconfiguration of twin models to accommodate **physical changes** (e.g., equipment upgrades) or **operational shifts** (e.g., new production lines).
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin (DT)** systems in the provided documents offer **multi-layered services** tailored to **operational optimization, predictive analytics, and closed-loop control**, categorized by **service type**, **target users**, and **capabilities**:

---

### **1. Monitoring Services (Real-Time and Historical)**
   - **Target Users**: **Manufacturing engineers, maintenance teams, quality control analysts, and operational managers**.
   - **Capabilities**:
     - **Real-time telemetry**: Continuous streaming of **sensor data** (e.g., vibration, temperature, current) from physical assets to the DT via **OPC UA, MQTT, or REST APIs** (Document [10], [12]).
     - **Anomaly detection**: AI-driven **unsupervised learning models** (e.g., autoencoders, isolation forests) to flag deviations in **battery health** (Document [17]) or **machine wear** (Document [9]).
     - **Historical trend analysis**: Integration with **time-series databases** (e.g., InfluxDB, TimescaleDB) to correlate **operational data** with **failure modes** (Document [14]).
     - **Energy monitoring**: Real-time tracking of **power consumption** in manufacturing processes to optimize **grid interactions** (Document [14]).
     - **Biophysical monitoring** (Document [7]): Tracking **microbial growth rates, pH levels, or oxygen saturation** in bioreactors via **IoT sensors**.

---

### **2. Predictive Services (Forecasting and Simulation)**
   - **Target Users**: **Production planners, supply chain managers, and R&D teams**.
   - **Capabilities**:
     - **Predictive maintenance**: **Machine learning models** (e.g., LSTM networks) trained on **vibration, temperature, and acoustic data** to predict **failure windows** (Document [9], [12]).
     - **Battery degradation forecasting** (Document [17]): **Physics-of-failure models** combined with **data-driven aging curves** to estimate **remaining useful life (RUL)** of battery cells.
     - **Yield prediction** (Document [7]): **Digital twins of bioprocesses** (e.g., fermentation) to forecast **product yield** based on **real-time biological parameters**.
     - **Demand-driven simulation**: **What-if analysis** for **production line reconfiguration** (e.g., switching between vehicle models) using **discrete-event simulation (DES)** tools (Document [10]).
     - **Traffic collision avoidance** (Document [16]): **On-orbit digital twins** for spacecraft to predict **debris trajectories** and optimize **maneuvering strategies**.

---

### **3. Optimization Services (Control and Decision Support)**
   - **Target Users**: **Process engineers, logistics teams, and AI/ML practitioners**.
   - **Capabilities**:
     - **Closed-loop control**: **Reinforcement learning (RL) agents** or **PID controllers** embedded in the DT to **adjust manufacturing parameters** (e.g., conveyor speeds, robot trajectories) in real-time (Document [8], [14]).
     - **Energy optimization**: **Model Predictive Control (MPC)** applied to **factory energy grids** to minimize **peak demand costs** (Document [14]).
     - **Resource allocation**: **Multi-objective optimization** for **raw material distribution** in automotive assembly lines (Document [9]).
     - **Battery thermal management**: **Optimal charging/discharging schedules** derived from DT simulations to prevent **thermal runaway** (Document [17]).
     - **Biophysical optimization** (Document [7]): **Dynamic adjustment of environmental conditions** (e.g., temperature, humidity) in bioreactors to maximize **yield** or **product purity**.

---

### **4. Visualization and Analytics Services**
   - **Target Users**: **Executive leadership, training programs, and end-users**.
   - **Capabilities**:
     - **3D/4D visualization**: **Interactive digital twins** rendered via **Unreal Engine, Unity, or WebGL** for **real-time monitoring** of **assembly lines** or **battery packs** (Document [8], [17]).
     - **Augmented Reality (AR)**: **AR overlays** (e.g., Microsoft HoloLens) to **guide maintenance technicians** with **step-by-step repair instructions** (Document [12]).
     - **Digital twin dashboards**: **Customizable dashboards** (e.g., Power BI, Tableau) with **KPIs** like **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)**, **energy efficiency metrics**, or **battery SoH** (Document [10]).
     - **Explainable AI (XAI)**: **SHAP values or LIME** to interpret **AI-driven predictions** (e.g., failure modes) for **non-technical stakeholders** (Document [15]).

---

### **5. Control and Automation Services**
   - **Target Users**: **Automation engineers, robotics teams**.
   - **Capabilities**:
     - **Digital twin-driven automation**: **PLC reprogramming** or **robot path planning** based on **real-time DT simulations** (Document [8]).
     - **Edge-based control**: **Low-latency decision-making** via **edge computing** (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson, AWS IoT Greengrass) for **critical manufacturing tasks** (Document [14]).
     - **Autonomous systems**: **Self-optimizing DTs** for **unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs)** or **autonomous robots** in logistics (Document [13]).

---

### **6. Compliance and Validation Services**
   - **Target Users**: **Regulatory bodies, quality assurance teams**.
   - **Capabilities**:
     - **Regulatory compliance validation**: **Digital twins validated against ISO 23247-1** to ensure **interoperability** and **traceability** of manufacturing processes (Document [10], [14]).
     - **Failure mode analysis**: **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** conducted on DT models to **identify critical failure points** (Document [9]).
     - **Certification support**: **Digital twins used in aerospace** (Document [16]) to validate **on-orbit collision avoidance algorithms** against **NASA/ESA standards**.

---

### **Cross-Cutting Service Enablers**
   - **Standardized APIs**: **RESTful or GraphQL APIs** for **third-party integration** (e.g., ERP systems like SAP) (Document [10]).
   - **Digital twin-as-a-service (DTaaS)**: **Cloud-based DT platforms** (e.g., Siemens MindSphere, PTC ThingWorx) for **scalable deployment** (Document [12]).
   - **Federated learning**: **Collaborative DT models** trained across **multiple factories** without sharing raw data (Document [13]).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **tooling and enablers** for Digital Twin (DT) systems in the provided documents are **highly specialized**, integrating **standardized frameworks, simulation engines, edge/Cloud platforms, and AI/ML tools**. Below is a **technical breakdown** by category:

---

### **1. Standardization Frameworks and Architectures**
   - **ISO/IEC 30173:2023** (Document [11]):
     - **Conceptual foundation**: Defines **core DT terminology** (e.g., *Digital Twin*, *Physical Twin*, *Digital Shadow*) and **ontological models** for **semantic interoperability**.
     - **Ontology-based modeling**: Uses **OWL (Web Ontology Language)** and **RDF (Resource Description Framework)** to **formalize relationships** between physical and digital entities.
   - **ISO 23247-1:2021** (Documents [10], [12], [14], [16]):
     - **Manufacturing-specific framework**: Structures DTs into **layers**:
       1. **Physical Layer**: IoT devices (e.g., **LoRaWAN, Zigbee sensors**).
       2. **Digital Layer**: **Simulation models** (e.g., **ANSYS, MATLAB/Simulink**).
       3. **Integration Layer**: **Middleware** (e.g., **OPC UA, MQTT brokers**).
       4. **Application Layer**: **User interfaces** (e.g., **3D visualization tools**).
     - **Modularity**: Supports **plug-and-play DT components** (e.g., **battery cells, robots**) via **standardized interfaces** (Document [17]).
   - **Systems of Twinned Systems (Document [13])**:
     - **Hierarchical DT architectures**: Enables **nested DTs** (e.g., **component → subsystem → system**) with **cross-system communication protocols** (e.g., **DDS, ROS 2**).

---

### **2. Simulation Engines and Modeling Tools**
   - **Physics-Based Simulation**:
     - **ANSYS Fluent**: **CFD (Computational Fluid Dynamics)** for **thermal management** in batteries (Document [17]).
     - **MATLAB/Simulink**: **Control system design** and **real-time simulation** for **automotive actuators** (Document [8]).
     - **COMSOL Multiphysics**: **Multiphysics modeling** (e.g., **electrochemical + thermal coupling** in batteries) (Document [17]).
   - **Data-Driven Simulation**:
     - **TensorFlow/PyTorch**: **Neural network models** for **predictive maintenance** (Document [9]).
     - **Scikit-learn**: **Traditional ML algorithms** (e.g., **Random Forests, Gradient Boosting**) for **failure prediction** (Document [12]).
   - **Digital Twin-Specific Tools**:
     - **Siemens Digital Twin Creator**: **Low-code platform** for **rapid DT prototyping** in manufacturing (Document [12]).
     - **PTC ThingWorx**: **Cloud-native DT platform** with **real-time analytics** (Document [12]).
     - **NVIDIA Omniverse**: **3D collaboration framework** for **shared DT environments** (Document [8]).

---

### **3. Edge and Cloud Computing Platforms**
   - **Edge Computing (Document [14])**:
     - **NVIDIA Jetson Xavier**: **On-premise edge deployment** for **low-latency DT control** (e.g., **robotics, autonomous systems**).
     - **AWS IoT Greengrass**: **Edge-to-Cloud synchronization** for **real-time DT updates**.
     - **Azure IoT Edge**: **Hybrid edge/Cloud processing** for **scalable DT analytics**.
   - **Cloud Platforms**:
     - **AWS IoT Core**: **Managed IoT connectivity** for **sensor data ingestion** (Document [12]).
     - **Google Cloud Platform (GCP)**: **BigQuery for time-series data** and **Vertex AI for ML training** (Document [15]).
     - **Microsoft Azure Digital Twins**: **Spatial DT modeling** with **GraphQL support** (Document [10]).

---

### **4. Data Management and Databases**
   - **Time-Series Databases**:
     - **InfluxDB**: **High-resolution sensor data storage** (e.g., **vibration, temperature**) (Document [14]).
     - **TimescaleDB**: **PostgreSQL extension** for **scalable time-series analytics** (Document [9]).
   - **Graph Databases**:
     - **Neo4j**: **Ontology storage** for **semantic relationships** in DTs (Document [11]).
   - **NoSQL Databases**:
     - **MongoDB**: **Flexible schema** for **unstructured DT metadata** (Document [12]).
   - **Data Lakes**:
     - **AWS S3 + Athena**: **Storage and querying** of **historical DT simulation data** (Document [15]).

---

### **5. Communication Protocols and Middleware**
   - **Industrial Protocols**:
     - **OPC UA**: **Secure, platform-independent** communication between **PLCs, sensors, and DTs** (Document [10], [12]).
     - **MQTT**: **Lightweight IoT messaging** for **low-bandwidth DT updates** (Document [14]).
     - **DDS (Data Distribution Service)**: **Real-time data sharing** for **distributed DT systems** (Document [13]).
   - **APIs and Integration**:
     - **RESTful APIs**: **Standardized endpoints** for **third-party DT integration** (e.g., **ERP systems**) (Document [10]).
     - **GraphQL**: **Flexible querying** for **DT data** (Document [10]).

---

### **6. AI/ML and Analytics Tools**
   - **Machine Learning Frameworks**:
     - **TensorFlow Extended (TFX)**: **ML pipeline orchestration** for **DT training** (Document [15]).
     - **Scikit-learn**: **Traditional ML** for **anomaly detection** (Document [9]).
   - **Deep Learning**:
     - **PyTorch Lightning**: **Scalable DL models** for **predictive analytics** (Document [15]).
   - **Explainable AI (XAI)**:
     - **SHAP, LIME**: **Interpretability tools** for **DT predictions** (Document [15]).
   - **Reinforcement Learning (RL)**:
     - **Stable Baselines3**: **RL agents** for **closed-loop DT control** (Document [8]).

---

### **7. Visualization and User Interface Tools**
   - **3D/4D Visualization**:
     - **Unreal Engine**: **Real-time 3D DT rendering** for **automotive manufacturing** (Document [8]).
     - **Blender + Python API**: **Custom DT visualizations** (Document [17]).
   - **AR/VR**:
     - **Microsoft HoloLens**: **AR overlays** for **maintenance guidance** (Document [12]).
   - **Dashboards**:
     - **Power BI**: **Interactive DT dashboards** with **KPIs** (Document [10]).
     - **Grafana**: **Customizable DT monitoring** (Document [14]).

---

### **8. Development and DevOps Tools**
   - **Version Control**:
     - **Git + GitHub/GitLab**: **DT model versioning** (Document [15]).
   - **CI/CD Pipelines**:
     - **Jenkins, GitHub Actions**: **Automated DT deployment** (Document [15]).
   - **Containerization**:
     - **Docker + Kubernetes**: **Scalable DT microservices** (Document [14]).

---

### **9. Specialized Enablers for Specific Domains**
   - **Automotive-Specific**:
     - **CATIA Digital Twin**: **PLM (Product Lifecycle Management) integration** for **vehicle design** (Document [8]).
     - **Siemens Teamcenter**: **Digital thread** for **end-to-end DT lifecycle** (Document [10]).
   - **Biophysical Systems**:
     - **COMSOL + MATLAB**: **Coupled biological-physical simulations** (Document [7]).
   - **Aerospace**:
     - **NASA’s OpenMDAO**: **Multidisciplinary DT optimization** (Document [16]).
   - **Battery Systems**:
     - **Battery Management Systems (BMS)**: **Hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) testing** with DTs (Document [17]).

---

### **10. Security and Compliance Tools**
   - **Zero-Trust Architecture**:
     - **OPC UA Security**: **Encryption (TLS 1.3) + mutual authentication** (Document [10]).
   - **Blockchain**:
     - **Hyperledger Fabric**: **Immutable DT audit logs** (Document [13]).
   - **Compliance Frameworks**:
     - **ISO 27001**: **DT data security standards** (Document [12]).
     - **FDA 21 CFR Part 11**: **Regulatory validation** for **biopharmaceutical DTs** (Document [7]).

---
**Cross-Cutting Observations**:
   - **Hybrid Cloud/Edge Deployment**: Most DTs leverage **edge for real-time control** and **Cloud for analytics** (Document [14]).
   - **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)**: **SysML, UML** used for **DT architecture design** (Document [15]).
   - **Federated Learning**: **Privacy-preserving DT training** across **multiple sites** (Document [13]).
   - **Digital Twin Marketplaces**: **Reusable DT components** (e.g., **battery models, robotics**) shared via **platforms like Siemens MindSphere** (Document [12]).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system enforces a **100 ms clock synchronization accuracy** between physical and digital components to enable computation offloading and coordination, aligning with vehicular application requirements. This temporal constraint is critical for safety-critical operations, as stricter synchronization (e.g., <100 ms) is mandated by standards for similar use cases. The system’s temporal granularity is dictated by hardware limitations, with no explicit mention of varying time scales for different services beyond the synchronization requirement. Latency requirements are implicitly tied to the 100 ms constraint, ensuring real-time responsiveness for data exchange between the Physical Twin Entity (e.g., sensors/actuators) and the Digital Twin Entity (e.g., data management and control).
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system follows a **decentralized, entity-based architecture** with three primary instances: the *Physical Twin Entity*, *Digital Twin Entity*, and *User Entity*, each deployed on distinct hardware components. This structure diverges from ISO 23247’s *Observation and Measurement Entity (OME)* by consolidating resource-specific and general automotive functionalities (e.g., *Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking*) within the Physical Twin Entity. The system does not explicitly support **multiple Digital Twin instances** or hierarchical multiplicities; instead, it emphasizes **functional decomposition** across entities with clear scope boundaries. Coordination between entities relies on ROS2 packages (one per entity) and ROS2 nodes, enabling modular communication but without explicit mention of hierarchical coordination mechanisms or shared responsibility models.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system adopts an **ISO 23247 Reference Architecture (RA)-inspired orchestration**, where software components are mapped to *Functional Entities (FEs)* and deployed as individual ROS2 packages. The architecture prioritizes **entity-based modularity**, with the *User Entity* hosting *3D Visualization* and *Control Panel* subcomponents (split from the standard *User Interface FE*), the *Digital Twin Entity* managing data and control logic, and the *Physical Twin Entity* handling pre-processing, actuation, and device communication. Resource management is implicit, with each entity deployed on separate hardware, impacting ownership and engineering processes. System-wide coordination is achieved via ROS2’s inter-node communication, though no explicit mention of orchestration frameworks (e.g., Kubernetes, edge orchestration) or dynamic resource allocation is provided.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin system adheres to the **ISO 23247** standard, which defines an **entity-based reference architecture (RA)** for manufacturing Digital Twins. The system comprises **Digital Twin Entities** that digitally represent **Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)**, including equipment, facilities, products, personnel, and processes. The computational models and data components are structured as follows:

- **Model Types**:
  - **Geometric Models**: Represent physical attributes of OMEs (e.g., equipment geometry, spatial layouts).
  - **Behavioral Models**: Simulate operational dynamics (e.g., process flows, equipment behavior under stress).
  - **Analytical Models**: Enable predictive analytics (e.g., performance forecasting, failure prediction).

- **Data Sources**:
  - Real-time sensor data from **Data Collection and Device Control Entities**.
  - Historical operational data (e.g., maintenance logs, production records).
  - External data feeds (e.g., environmental conditions, supply chain data).

- **Data Management Approaches**:
  - **Entity-Based Partitioning**: Each **Functional Entity (FE)** manages specific tasks (e.g., data transformation, user interfacing).
  - **OPC UA (OPC Unified Architecture)**: Mentioned as a potential implementation for interfacing FEs, enabling standardized data exchange and control protocols.
  - **Decentralized Data Handling**: FEs may operate independently, with **Digital Twin Entities** aggregating and synchronizing data.

- **Model Relationships**:
  - **OME-Digital Twin Entity Coupling**: Bidirectional synchronization between physical OMEs and their digital representations.
  - **Cross-Entity Interoperability**: FEs interact via defined interfaces (e.g., OPC UA) to ensure cohesive system behavior.

- **Specific Roles**:
  - **Data Collection and Device Control Entities**: Bridge physical sensors/actuators and Digital Twin Entities, ensuring real-time data flow.
  - **User Entities**: Provide human-machine interfaces (HMIs) for monitoring (e.g., dashboards) and control (e.g., remote actuation).
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components:
The **physical acting components** in this Digital Twin system are governed by the **Data Collection and Device Control Entity** as per **ISO 23247**. Key technical details include:

- **Types of Actuators**:
  - Not explicitly specified in the documents, but implied to include **industrial actuators** (e.g., motors, valves, robotic arms) for equipment control.

- **Control Capabilities**:
  - **Remote Actuation**: Enabled via **Functional Entities (FEs)** interfacing with OMEs, allowing centralized or decentralized control.
  - **Process Automation**: Actuators execute predefined or dynamically adjusted commands (e.g., adjusting production speeds, activating cooling systems).

- **Remote Control Interfaces**:
  - **OPC UA**: Likely used for standardized communication between control systems and Digital Twin Entities, ensuring interoperability.
  - **User Entity Interfaces**: Provide operators with tools to issue control commands (e.g., start/stop machinery, adjust parameters).

- **Actuation Ranges and Limitations**:
  - Not detailed in the documents, but constrained by **physical OME specifications** (e.g., motor torque limits, valve flow rates).

- **Safety Constraints**:
  - Implicitly addressed via **validation mechanisms** (see *fidelity_and_validity_considerations*), ensuring actuators comply with operational safety protocols.
  - **Error Handling**: Likely integrated into FE logic to prevent unsafe states (e.g., emergency stops).

- **Addressing Mechanisms**:
  - **Entity-Based Addressing**: Actuators are addressed via their corresponding **Digital Twin Entity** and **Functional Entity**, ensuring precise control mapping.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components:
The **physical sensing infrastructure** is integral to the **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**, as defined by **ISO 23247**. Key technical specifications include:

- **Sensor Types**:
  - Not explicitly enumerated, but inferred to include:
    - **Industrial Sensors**: Temperature, pressure, vibration, flow rate, and position sensors for equipment monitoring.
    - **Environmental Sensors**: Humidity, ambient temperature, and air quality sensors for facility-level data.
    - **Process Sensors**: Sensors embedded in production lines (e.g., machine tool sensors, conveyor belt sensors).

- **Measurement Capabilities**:
  - **High-Resolution Data**: Capable of capturing fine-grained operational metrics (e.g., sub-millimeter displacement, microsecond timing).
  - **Multi-Parameter Monitoring**: Simultaneous collection of physical, chemical, and operational parameters (e.g., torque + temperature + speed).

- **Spatial Distribution**:
  - **Distributed Network**: Sensors deployed across **OMEs** (e.g., sensors on individual machines, in production cells, or within facilities).
  - **Hierarchical Placement**: Macro-level sensors (e.g., facility-wide) and micro-level sensors (e.g., component-specific).

- **Sampling Frequencies**:
  - Not specified, but typical industrial sensors operate at **10 Hz to 1 kHz** for dynamic processes (e.g., CNC machines) or **lower rates (1 Hz)** for stable environmental conditions.

- **Data Transmission Rates**:
  - **Real-Time Requirements**: Data transmitted via **OPC UA** or similar protocols to ensure low-latency communication (e.g., <100 ms for critical control loops).
  - **Bandwidth Considerations**: Compressed or prioritized data streams for high-volume sensor networks.

- **Accuracy and Precision Specifications**:
  - **High Precision**: Sensors likely meet **ISO 9000/9001** or **IEC 61508** standards for industrial applications (e.g., ±0.1% accuracy for critical measurements).
  - **Calibration Mechanisms**: Periodic recalibration to maintain precision (e.g., automated calibration routines).

- **Data Collection Mechanisms**:
  - **Automated Data Acquisition**: Sensors interface directly with **Functional Entities** for real-time processing.
  - **Edge Processing**: Pre-filtering or aggregation at the sensor level to reduce network load.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
The **fidelity and validity** of the Digital Twin system are ensured through **ISO 23247** compliance and implicit methodologies outlined in the standard. Key considerations include:

- **Model Fidelity Requirements**:
  - **High-Resolution Digital Representations**: Digital Twin Entities must accurately mirror **Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)**, including geometric, behavioral, and analytical fidelity.
  - **Dynamic Updates**: Continuous synchronization with real-time sensor data to maintain fidelity (e.g., adjusting models for wear-and-tear effects).

- **Validation and Verification Methods**:
  - **Comparative Analysis**: Cross-checking digital models against physical OMEs (e.g., validating geometric models via 3D scanning).
  - **Simulation Testing**: Running Digital Twin simulations under controlled conditions to verify behavioral accuracy (e.g., failure mode simulations).
  - **Benchmarking**: Comparing model outputs against historical operational data for consistency.

- **Uncertainty Sources and Quantification**:
  - **Sensor Noise**: Quantified via **signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)** metrics for each sensor type.
  - **Model Approximations**: Uncertainty in analytical models due to simplifications (e.g., turbulence modeling in fluid dynamics).
  - **Environmental Variability**: External factors (e.g., temperature fluctuations) introducing variability in OME behavior.

- **Error Detection and Correction Mechanisms**:
  - **Anomaly Detection**: Functional Entities employ **machine learning or statistical methods** to identify deviations (e.g., outlier detection in sensor data).
  - **Automated Correction**: Self-correcting models (e.g., recalibrating sensors or adjusting digital representations via feedback loops).
  - **Human-in-the-Loop**: User Entities provide tools for manual validation (e.g., operator overrides for suspected errors).

- **Quality Assurance Processes**:
  - **ISO 23247 Compliance Audits**: Regular assessments to ensure adherence to the standard’s entity-based architecture.
  - **Continuous Integration/Deployment (CI/CD)**: Automated pipelines for updating Digital Twin models with validated data.
  - **Documentation Standards**: Traceability of model changes and data sources for auditing purposes.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **Physical-to-Virtual Interaction** in this Digital Twin System (DTS) involves real-time bidirectional data flows from physical entities (e.g., LiDAR, motor, steering servo, and vehicle state sensors) to the digital twin. Key components and processes include:

1. **Data Types Transmitted**:
   - **Raw Sensor Data**: LiDAR point cloud data (2D/3D point representations), vehicle state (e.g., speed, acceleration), motor/steering servo actuation signals, and point cloud clustering/tracking metadata.
   - **Processed Data**: Object tracks, projected driving paths, and clustered point cloud segments (output from the *Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking* SW component).

2. **Communication Protocols**:
   - **Inter-Entity Communication**: Wi-Fi network using **UDP** at the transport layer for physical-to-digital data transmission.
   - **Intra-Entity Communication**: **ROS2** (Robot Operating System 2) for internal software component communication within the Digital Twin Entity.

3. **Transmission Frequencies**:
   - Not explicitly quantified in the document, but implied to be **high-frequency** (e.g., ≥10Hz for LiDAR/vehicle state data) to support real-time monitoring and visualization.

4. **Event Triggers**:
   - **Continuous Data Streams**: LiDAR and sensor data are transmitted as continuous streams for real-time processing.
   - **Event-Driven Updates**: Object tracks and driving path projections are dynamically updated based on processed sensor inputs.

5. **Data Quality Assurance**:
   - **Preprocessing Steps**: Raw LiDAR point clouds undergo *Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking* preprocessing (FE: Feature Extraction) to segment and track objects in the environment.
   - **Data Translation**: The **Data Translation FE** (ISO 23247 compliant) serializes data, aligns message types, and performs **unit conversions** for consistency across streams.

6. **Data Integration Mechanisms**:
   - **ROS2 Topics**: Sensor data is published as ROS2 topics for real-time consumption by visualization and reporting components.
   - **3D Visualization Integration**: Processed data (e.g., object tracks, driving paths) are rendered in real-time via the *3D Visualization* component, enabling user feedback loops.

The system ensures **low-latency** data propagation by leveraging UDP for inter-entity communication and ROS2 for intra-entity synchronization, with no explicit mention of compression or error correction mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The **Virtual-to-Physical Interaction** in this DTS involves control commands generated by the digital twin entity (e.g., ACC - Adaptive Cruise Control) and transmitted to physical actuators. Key aspects include:

1. **Command Types**:
   - **Actuation Commands**: Motor and steering servo actuation signals derived from the *Actuation FE* (Feature Extraction) component.
   - **Control Signals**: Generated by the **ACC Controlling FE** (ISO 23247 compliant) to regulate vehicle speed and trajectory based on real-time sensor data and projected paths.

2. **Control Message Formats**:
   - **Data Translation**: Commands are serialized and translated via the **Data Translation FE**, ensuring standardized message types and unit consistency (e.g., converting between sensor units and actuator control signals).
   - **ROS2 Topics**: Control signals are published as ROS2 topics for internal processing before transmission.

3. **Validation and Authentication Mechanisms**:
   - **Not explicitly documented**, but implied to rely on the **Data Translation FE** for message integrity (serialization/deserialization) and potential **ROS2 security plugins** (e.g., authentication for inter-entity communication).

4. **Feedback Systems**:
   - **Closed-Loop Control**: The ACC system uses real-time sensor feedback (e.g., LiDAR object tracks, vehicle state) to adjust control signals dynamically.
   - **Visualization Feedback**: The *3D Visualization* component displays projected driving paths and object interactions, aiding human-in-the-loop validation.

5. **Conflict Resolution**:
   - **Not explicitly detailed**, but assumed to be handled via **priority-based command processing** in the *Controlling FE* (e.g., emergency braking overriding cruise control).

6. **Command Execution Confirmation**:
   - **Not explicitly documented**, but implied to rely on **acknowledgment mechanisms** within the ROS2 network or UDP-based feedback loops from physical actuators (e.g., servo position confirmation).

The system does not specify explicit **command timeout** or **retry mechanisms**, but the use of UDP suggests potential for packet loss, which may require higher-layer recovery in the *Controlling FE*.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **Digital Twin Technical Connection** infrastructure is designed for real-time, low-latency communication between physical, digital, and user entities. Key technical details include:

1. **Communication Protocols**:
   - **Inter-Entity (Physical ↔ Digital)**: **UDP** over Wi-Fi for sensor data and control command transmission (stateless, low-overhead).
   - **Intra-Entity (Digital Components)**: **ROS2** for internal software component communication (topic-based, publish-subscribe model).

2. **Network Topology and Architecture**:
   - **Decentralized**: Physical Twin Entity (sensors/actuators) communicates directly with the Digital Twin Entity via Wi-Fi.
   - **ROS2 Network**: Internal components (e.g., *Data Translation FE*, *Controlling FE*) communicate via ROS2 domains/topics, enabling modular scalability.

3. **Bandwidth and Latency Requirements**:
   - **Not explicitly quantified**, but implied to require **≥10Mbps** for LiDAR point cloud streams (assuming 10Hz, 1M points/frame at 10 bytes/point).
   - **Latency Target**: Sub-100ms for real-time control loops (UDP latency typically <5ms for local Wi-Fi; ROS2 overhead adds ~10–30ms).

4. **Reliability and Redundancy Mechanisms**:
   - **No explicit redundancy** documented (e.g., no failover protocols or duplicate sensors).
   - **ROS2 QoS Policies**: May include **reliability settings** (e.g., `best_effort` or `reliable`) for critical control topics, though not specified.

5. **Security Protocols**:
   - **Not explicitly detailed**, but implied to include:
     - **Data Translation FE**: Ensures message integrity via serialization/deserialization.
     - **Wi-Fi Security**: Standard Wi-Fi encryption (WPA2/WPA3) assumed for inter-entity communication.
     - **ROS2 Security**: Potential use of **ROS2 security plugins** (e.g., authentication, encryption) for intra-entity communication, though not confirmed.

The architecture prioritizes **performance over strict reliability**, with UDP chosen for low-latency sensor data transmission and ROS2 for structured internal communication.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **Digital Twin Hosting and Deployment** infrastructure supports real-time processing and visualization with the following characteristics:

1. **Hosting Platforms**:
   - **Edge Deployment**: Physical Twin Entity (sensors/actuators) and Digital Twin Entity (ROS2-based components) are co-located or deployed on **edge devices** (e.g., onboard vehicle compute units).
   - **No Cloud Reference**: The system does not mention cloud hosting; deployment is **on-premise/edge-centric**.

2. **Computational Requirements**:
   - **Sensor Processing**: *Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking* FE requires **GPU acceleration** (implied for real-time LiDAR processing).
   - **Control Logic**: *Controlling FE* and *Data Translation FE* run on **CPU-bound** tasks (serialization, unit conversion).
   - **Visualization**: *3D Visualization* component demands **high-end GPU** for real-time rendering of point clouds and object tracks.

3. **Storage Requirements**:
   - **Temporary Storage**: In-memory buffers for ROS2 topics and real-time data streams (no persistent storage mentioned).
   - **Reporting Data**: *Reporting FE* generates reports stored in **local data storage** (format/type unspecified).

4. **Scalability Mechanisms**:
   - **Modular ROS2 Architecture**: Components can be scaled horizontally by adding more nodes (e.g., redundant *Data Translation FEs*).
   - **No Distributed Computing**: No mention of Kubernetes, Docker swarms, or cloud-native orchestration; scaling is limited to edge devices.

5. **Deployment Models Supported**:
   - **Single-Node Deployment**: All components (Physical, Digital, User) are tightly integrated on a **single vehicle platform**.
   - **No Multi-Entity Scaling**: Not designed for distributed Digital Twin deployments across multiple vehicles or geographic locations.

6. **Availability/Reliability Assurance**:
   - **No Redundancy**: Single points of failure for critical components (e.g., LiDAR sensor, *Controlling FE*).
   - **Fault Tolerance**: Relies on **ROS2 QoS policies** (if configured) and **UDP retransmission** (none guaranteed) for resilience.
   - **No SLA Documentation**: No service-level agreements (SLAs) or uptime guarantees specified.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The lifecycle of the Digital Twin (DT) system, as outlined in **ISO 23247 Part 2 (Reference Architecture)**, follows an **entity-based framework** where each **Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)**—such as equipment, facilities, products, or processes—undergoes structured lifecycle phases. The architecture mandates four primary **entities** (Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, User Entity) and **Functional Entities (FEs)**, which decompose DT functionality into modular tasks (e.g., data actuation, human-interpretable transformation, or user interfacing).

**Lifecycle Phases and Representations:**
1. **Conceptualization Phase**: Defines OMEs and their observable attributes (physical presence/operations) while mapping them to **Digital Twin Entities (DTEs)**. The DTEs are initialized with **digital representations** of the OME’s physical state, parameters, and behaviors, adhering to the **entity-based RA** principles.
2. **Development Phase**: Involves **functional decomposition** into FEs, where each FE implements specific tasks (e.g., OPC UA integration for device control or data transformation). The standard does not prescribe implementation methods but suggests **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)** as a viable protocol for interoperability between entities.
3. **Deployment Phase**: The DT system is deployed with **real-time data synchronization** between the **Data Collection and Device Control Entity** (collecting sensor/actuator data) and the DTE. User interfaces (provided by the **User Entity**) enable monitoring and control via human-interpretable formats.
4. **Operation Phase**: Continuous **data-driven updates** to the DTE reflect changes in the OME’s physical state. The lifecycle includes **transition management** between phases via **version control** (implicitly implied for FE updates) and **governance frameworks** to ensure alignment with manufacturing objectives.

**Lifecycle Governance**: The ISO 23247 framework emphasizes **modularity and separation of concerns**—each entity operates independently but collaboratively, with governance ensuring consistency in data exchange and representation fidelity. Transition between phases is managed through **entity-specific validation** (e.g., FE compliance checks) and **information exchange standards** (e.g., Part 4 of ISO 23247).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The **twinning process** for this Digital Twin (DT) system is governed by the **ISO 23247 Reference Architecture (Part 2)**, which defines a **methodology centered on entity decomposition** and **functional modularity**. The process involves:

**Engineering Methodologies**:
- **Entity-Based Development**: The DT is constructed using four primary entities (**Data Collection and Device Control Entity**, **Digital Twin Entity (DTE)**, **User Entity**, and **Functional Entities (FEs)**). Each entity is responsible for a distinct role, with FEs handling granular tasks (e.g., data actuation, transformation, or user interaction). The standard does not mandate specific implementation tools but **suggests OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)** for interoperability between entities.
- **Observable Manufacturing Element (OME) Mapping**: The process begins by identifying OMEs (e.g., equipment, processes) and mapping them to DTEs, ensuring the digital representation aligns with physical attributes and operations.

**Quality Assurance and Testing**:
- **Functional Validation**: Each FE undergoes validation to ensure it fulfills its designated task (e.g., accurate data transformation or user interface responsiveness). The standard implies **modular testing** to verify entity interactions and data consistency.
- **Data Consistency Checks**: The **Data Collection and Device Control Entity** validates sensor/actuator data before transmission to the DTE, ensuring real-time fidelity.

**Version Control and Evolution**:
- **Modular Updates**: The DT evolves through **FE-specific updates**, allowing incremental improvements without disrupting the entire system. For example, a FE responsible for OPC UA communication can be upgraded independently.
- **Backward Compatibility**: The architecture supports **evolution management** by maintaining clear interfaces between entities, ensuring new FEs integrate seamlessly with existing components. The standard does not specify version control tools but emphasizes **entity-based isolation** to manage changes.

**Testing and Validation Approaches**:
- **Entity-Level Testing**: Each entity (e.g., User Entity for interface validation) is tested independently before integration.
- **End-to-End Validation**: The system undergoes **real-time data synchronization tests** to confirm consistency between the OME and DTE. The **ISO 23247 Part 4 (Information Exchange)** likely provides guidelines for validation protocols, though specifics are not detailed in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The Digital Twin (DT) system adheres to the **ISO 23247 standard**, a comprehensive framework for manufacturing DTs, structured into four parts:
1. **Overview and General Principles** (Part 1): Establishes foundational concepts for DTs in manufacturing.
2. **Reference Architecture** (Part 2): Defines an **entity-based RA** with four primary entities (**Data Collection and Device Control Entity**, **Digital Twin Entity**, **User Entity**) and **Functional Entities (FEs)** for modular task execution. This part mandates **interoperability** between entities via unspecified but implied standards.
3. **Digital Representation of Physical Manufacturing Elements** (Part 3): Specifies how **Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)** (e.g., equipment, processes) are digitally represented, ensuring alignment between physical and virtual states.
4. **Information Exchange** (Part 4): Likely outlines **data exchange protocols** and **semantic interoperability** requirements, though specifics are not detailed in the provided documents.

**Compliance and Interoperability Standards**:
- **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)**: Implicitly recommended in Part 2 for **device control and data collection**, ensuring standardized communication between the **Data Collection and Device Control Entity** and other components.
- **Entity-Based Modularity**: The architecture enforces **modular compliance** by isolating functionality into FEs, each adhering to entity-specific roles (e.g., data transformation, user interaction).
- **Certification Requirements**: While not explicitly stated, compliance with **ISO 23247** implies adherence to **manufacturing DT best practices**, though formal certification frameworks are not detailed.

**Interoperability**:
- The system leverages **entity interfaces** to enable seamless data flow between components, with **OPC UA** as a potential enabler for cross-platform compatibility. The **ISO 23247 Part 4** likely defines **data exchange formats** (e.g., JSON, XML) and **semantic models** for consistent interpretation across entities.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations:
The security and safety considerations for the Digital Twin system, as referenced in the provided documents, are primarily aligned with **ISO 26262-1:2018** for functional safety in road vehicles and **ISO 15622:2018** for adaptive cruise control systems. Key technical measures include:

1. **Functional Safety (ISO 26262-1:2018)**:
   - Adherence to **ASIL (Automotive Safety Integrity Level)** classification to ensure risk mitigation in safety-critical systems, with explicit requirements for hardware, software, and system-level safety measures.
   - Implementation of **fail-safe mechanisms** to prevent hazardous states, including redundant system designs, error detection, and recovery procedures.
   - **Risk assessment** via **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** and **FTA (Fault Tree Analysis)** to identify and mitigate potential failure modes in the Digital Twin’s safety-critical components.
   - **Cybersecurity protocols** integrated into safety mechanisms, ensuring secure communication and data integrity for V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) services, as referenced in **3GPP TS 22.185** for adaptive cruise control systems.
   - **Access control mechanisms** with role-based authentication to restrict unauthorized modifications or data access, particularly for systems interacting with real-time vehicle operations.

2. **Adaptive Cruise Control (ISO 15622:2018)**:
   - **Performance requirements** mandate robust **threat mitigation strategies**, including collision avoidance and fail-safe braking systems, to ensure safety in dynamic driving scenarios.
   - **Test procedures** for validation of safety-critical behaviors, such as emergency deceleration and lane-keeping, under simulated and real-world conditions.

3. **Model-Driven Architecture (Dalibor et al., 2020)**:
   - **Interactive Digital Twin cockpits** leverage **model-driven engineering (MDE)** principles to ensure traceability and consistency in safety-critical system modeling, reducing human error in design and implementation.
   - **Safety-by-design** approaches are embedded in the **model transformation pipelines**, ensuring compliance with safety standards from the earliest stages of development.

4. **V2X Security (3GPP TS 22.185)**:
   - **Authentication systems** for secure communication between vehicles and infrastructure, including **public-key cryptography** and **digital certificates** to verify the authenticity of messages.
   - **Data integrity checks** via cryptographic hashing (e.g., HMAC) to prevent tampering with safety-critical data exchanged in the Digital Twin system.
   - **Confidentiality protections** for sensitive data, such as vehicle telemetry, through **end-to-end encryption** during transmission and storage.

These measures collectively ensure that the Digital Twin system adheres to **ISO 26262-1:2018** safety standards while integrating **cybersecurity best practices** for V2X applications, as outlined in **3GPP TS 22.185** and **ISO 15622:2018**.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture
